steps.title: Identify what the comparison must show
steps.content: The passage claims e-books succeeded with fiction genres (read straight through) but were significantly less successful with nonfiction reference genres (flip back and forth). The blank asks for a comparison that illustrates this claim. The comparison must pit a fiction genre against a nonfiction/reference genre in the same time period — and the fiction genre's e-book share must be higher.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
Test each choice against the requirement of a fiction-vs.-reference cross-genre comparison:
  • Choice A — '2016 cookbook vs. 2016 science fiction and fantasy': cookbooks are a nonfiction reference genre (flip back and forth); science fiction and fantasy is a straight-through fiction genre. The 2016 e-book share for sci-fi/fantasy (36.7%) is 3.5 times higher than for cookbooks (10.5%) — directly illustrates the claim. ✓
  • Choice B — '2006 romance vs. 2016 romance': both data points are the same genre (romance), so this shows growth over time within one fiction genre, not a fiction-vs.-reference contrast. ✗
  • Choice C — '2006 romance vs. 2006 science fiction and fantasy': both romance and sci-fi/fantasy are straight-through fiction genres — no reference genre to compare against, so the claim cannot be illustrated. ✗
  • Choice D — '2011 travel guides vs. 2016 travel guides': both data points are the same genre (travel guides), showing growth within one nonfiction genre over time, not a cross-genre comparison. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
